content: Coronary artery disease (CAD) is caused by atherosclerotic plaque buildup in coronary arteries, leading to myocardial ischemia. Pathophysiology: endothelial dysfunction allows LDL cholesterol infiltration into intima, oxidation, macrophage recruitment forming foam cells, fatty streak development, smooth muscle cell migration, fibrous cap formation. Vulnerable plaques have thin caps, large lipid cores, and inflammation—prone to rupture causing acute thrombosis (MI). Risk factors: modifiable (smoking, hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes, obesity, physical inactivity, unhealthy diet), non-modifiable (age, male sex, family history, genetics), emerging (Lp(a), hs-CRP, social determinants). ASCVD risk calculators (Pooled Cohort Equations, SCORE2) estimate 10-year risk to guide prevention. Clinical presentations: stable angina (predictable chest pain with exertion), ACS (unstable angina, NSTEMI, STEMI), silent ischemia, heart failure, arrhythmias, sudden cardiac death.

Stable CAD diagnosis: clinical assessment (pretest probability based on age, sex, symptom typicality), resting ECG (may show prior MI, LVH, ST-T changes), echocardiogram (wall motion abnormalities, EF). Functional testing for ischemia: exercise ECG (if able to exercise and interpretable ECG), stress echocardiography (exercise or dobutamine), nuclear perfusion imaging (SPECT or PET with exercise or vasodilator), stress cardiac MRI. Anatomic testing: coronary CT angiography (CCTA) excellent for ruling out CAD in low-intermediate risk patients (high negative predictive value), can characterize plaque; invasive coronary angiography gold standard, allows immediate PCI if needed. ISCHEMIA trial showed no difference in CV outcomes between initial invasive vs conservative strategy in stable CAD with moderate-severe ischemia, supporting medical therapy first-line. FFR (fractional flow reserve) or iFR during angiography determines physiologic significance of intermediate lesions (FFR ≤0.80 indicates ischemia-producing stenosis).

Stable CAD management: lifestyle modifications (smoking cessation, Mediterranean diet, exercise, weight management) plus optimal medical therapy. Antianginal medications: beta-blockers (first-line), calcium channel blockers, long-acting nitrates, ranolazine (late sodium current inhibitor). Secondary prevention: high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80mg or rosuvastatin 20-40mg), antiplatelet therapy (aspirin 75-100mg daily; clopidogrel if aspirin intolerant), ACEi/ARB (especially with HTN, DM, CKD, HFrEF), beta-blocker post-MI or with HFrEF. Revascularization indications: symptom control despite OMT, left main disease, multivessel CAD with reduced EF, large area of ischemia. PCI preferred for single-vessel or focal disease; DES (drug-eluting stents) standard. CABG preferred for left main disease, multivessel CAD with diabetes (FREEDOM trial), complex multivessel disease (higher SYNTAX score), or concomitant valve surgery. CABG provides better long-term patency and fewer repeat revascularizations but higher perioperative risk. DAPT duration after PCI: 6-12 months depending on bleeding/ischemic risk.
